past_medical_history: Remote history of mild depressive episode 8 years ago, resolved
family_history: Mother had dementia diagnosed at age 78, passed at 84
Father had coronary artery disease, died of myocardial infarction at 69
One brother, age 75, hypertension and type 2 diabetes

social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband of 48 years in a single-story home
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, approximately 2-3 per week
social_history.exercise: Used to walk daily, has become less active over the past year

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; oriented to month and year but not exact date. MMSE 22/30, losing points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes), orientation (2 points lost), and serial sevens (2 errors). Clock drawing shows mildly disorganized number placement with hands set to incorrect time. Unable to recall 3 words after 5 minutes even with categorical cues. Naming intact for high-frequency items but hesitates on low-frequency items (e.g., 'stethoscope'). Repetition intact. Comprehension intact for simple commands, mild difficulty with multi-step commands.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Face symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. No dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal base, normal stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflex. No myoclonus. Anosmia not tested.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss over the past two years, worsening over the last six months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Margaret Thornton is a 72-year-old right-handed retired elementary school teacher who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by her husband, Robert, for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties over the past two years. Her husband reports that he first began noticing subtle changes approximately two years ago when she started repeating questions she had asked minutes earlier and occasionally forgetting recent conversations with family members. Over the past year, the symptoms have become more pronounced. She has missed two scheduled hair salon appointments, which her husband describes as very unlike her, as she has kept the same standing appointment for over twenty years. She has difficulty recalling the names of acquaintances she has known for years, though she recognizes their faces. At their weekly bridge club, which she has attended for fifteen years, her partner recently commented that Margaret seems to lose track of which cards have been played, something that never happened before. Her husband also notes that she has become more reliant on written lists for grocery shopping, whereas she previously managed the household shopping from memory. She left a pot of water boiling on the stove unattended on two occasions in the past three months, prompting her husband to begin monitoring the kitchen more closely. She has not gotten lost while driving on familiar routes, but her husband has taken over driving on longer trips because she seemed anxious navigating highways. She continues to dress and groom herself independently and manages her personal hygiene without difficulty. She is aware that her memory is not what it used to be and appears frustrated and mildly anxious about the changes. There have been no episodes of confusion, hallucinations, personality changes, or behavioral disturbances. She denies headache, seizures, falls, tremor, or difficulty walking. Sleep has been unremarkable, with no REM sleep behavior reported by her husband. Her mood has been somewhat subdued but she denies persistent sadness or anhedonia.

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent hippocampal fissures bilaterally with widened choroidal fissures
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality within hippocampal formations

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Increased CSF spaces surrounding medial temporal structures bilaterally, left slightly greater than right

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci in temporal and parietal convexities out of proportion to patient age

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 2-3 bilaterally
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Mild lateral ventriculomegaly consistent with ex vacuo dilatation secondary to parenchymal volume loss
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in temporal and parietal regions

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA
mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal lobe and hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 2-3) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss. No acute intracranial pathology. Age-appropriate periventricular white matter changes (Fazekas grade 1). Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 135 mEq/L (L), Glucose 103 mg/dL (H).

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 22/30 points (H), MoCA 18/30 points (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall 5th percentile percentile (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall 1st percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 10th percentile percentile (H), Trail Making Test Part B 20th percentile percentile (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test Part B: 20th percentile percentile (H)

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake, most prominent in prefrontal, precuneus, and lateral temporoparietal cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Diffuse bilateral cortical binding with relative sparing of occipital and sensorimotor cortex

additional_observations: White matter non-specific uptake within normal limits
No focal asymmetry to suggest alternative pathology
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. The distribution and intensity of tracer uptake are typical of moderate amyloid burden. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex and posterior cingulate/precuneus

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Significantly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with relative preservation of primary sensorimotor cortex, occipital cortex, basal ganglia, and cerebellum

additional_observations: Primary visual cortex metabolism preserved, arguing against posterior cortical atrophy variant
Basal ganglia and thalamic metabolism normal
output.impression: No findings to suggest frontotemporal dementia or Lewy body dementia. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 13 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 37.7 mg/dL. Glucose: 62.9 mg/dL (ratio 0.63 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 385 pg/mL (reference >500 pg/mL) - DECREASED. Amyloid_beta_40: 8200 pg/mL (reference range 5000-12000 pg/mL) - Normal. Amyloid_beta_42_40_ratio: 0.047 (reference >0.065) - DECREASED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 78 pg/mL (reference <60 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. Total_tau: 485 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7.5-8 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, below the normal range of 8.5-12 Hz

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, left greater than right

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not significantly obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age and cooperation

output.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7.5-8 Hz with intermittent bitemporal theta slowing, left greater than right. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation was not performed. Recording duration was 30 minutes in the awake state only.

interactions: No significant interactions with lisinopril, atorvastatin, or acetaminophen
warnings: May cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate especially in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) common at initiation, usually transient
Use caution with concurrent anticholinergic medications as they may reduce efficacy
alternatives: Rivastigmine (patch or oral) - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor

differential.key_distinguishing: MMSE 22/30 is below normal range; neuropsych testing shows impairment beyond normal aging; functional decline confirmed by family
Gradual progressive decline rather than stepwise; minimal vascular risk factors; MRI shows only Fazekas grade 1 white matter changes; memory-predominant rather than executive-predominant pattern
Gradual onset over 2 years; GDS 4/15 does not support significant depression; patient makes effort on testing; cognitive deficits on neuropsych testing are disproportionate to mood symptoms
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism on exam, no REM sleep behavior disorder, no fluctuating cognition reported
No prominent behavioral or personality changes; memory impairment is the predominant symptom rather than executive or language deficits; preserved social decorum

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, including timeline, nature of cognitive complaints, functional status, and behavioral symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 2 years with functional impairment confirmed by spouse

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including detailed mental status assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 22/30 with prominent recall deficit; neurological exam otherwise unremarkable

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All within normal limits, ruling out reversible causes of cognitive decline

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2-3) and temporoparietal cortical volume loss
Amnestic profile with severely impaired delayed recall, reduced semantic fluency, consistent with Alzheimer disease

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers or amyloid PET for pathological confirmation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau confirming AD pathology (A+T+N+)

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family, including discussion of prognosis, safety planning, advance directives, and community resources
Refer to occupational therapy for cognitive strategies and home safety evaluation
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to assess treatment response and disease progression

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia (labs: TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from family member or informant
Assess functional status and safety (driving, cooking, medication management)
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin) which worsen cognitive function
Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety without careful consideration of cognitive side effects
Attributing symptoms to normal aging without formal evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy in mild disease (not indicated until moderate stage)
Ordering brain biopsy (not indicated given clinical and biomarker profile)
Prescribing antipsychotics without clear behavioral indication (risk of increased mortality in dementia)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 2 years with insidious onset is the hallmark presentation of early Alzheimer disease
Functional decline (missed appointments, kitchen safety concerns, reliance on lists) distinguishes dementia from mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 22/30 with disproportionate recall deficit (0/3 at 5 minutes) is characteristic of hippocampal-type amnesia seen in AD
Normal neurological exam aside from cognitive deficits argues against vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, and frontotemporal dementia
Family history of dementia in mother is a risk factor supporting the diagnosis
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 2-3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss is the classic neuroimaging pattern for AD
Normal dementia screening labs rule out treatable causes and support a primary neurodegenerative diagnosis
The straightforward nature of this case lies in the classic amnestic presentation, supportive family history, consistent MRI findings, and absence of features suggesting alternative diagnoses

key_educational_points: Early AD presents with progressive episodic memory loss as the predominant symptom
Reversible causes of dementia must always be excluded with laboratory testing
Hippocampal atrophy on MRI is a supportive but not pathognomonic finding
CSF biomarkers (Abeta42, phospho-tau, total tau) can confirm AD pathology in vivo
Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line pharmacotherapy for mild-to-moderate AD
